coredata.dc:title: Flowing electrolytic synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and carbon nanosheets
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yao, Su
Hu, Yufei
Li, Gongke
coredata.dc:description: A flowing electrolytic device equipped with large electrode area and high electric field intensity was designed for the preparation of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) and carbon nanosheets (CNSs). Comparing with normal electrolysis, the flowing electrolysis showed higher efficiency for the preparation and separation of the products. In the high intensity electric field, the CNPs and CNSs were enriched to the flowing electrolyte and the anode surface respectively. The obtained CNPs were regarded as a fluorescent probe of Fe3+, and the CNSs modified electrode was fabricated to be an electrochemical sensor of acetaminophen for its electrocatalytic properties. So the CNPs and CNSs were expected for the application of fluorescent and electrochemical detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: flowing electrolysis
carbon nanoparticles
carbon nanosheets

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The design of the flowing electrolytic device
Properties of the CNPs and CNSs
Potential application of the CNPs and CNSs

Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the flowing electrolytic device.

The HRTEM images of flowing electrolyte (A) and purge fluid (B) which was obtained in the electrolytic current of 10mA, and the HRTEM images of flowing electrolyte (C) and purge fluid (D) which was obtained in the electrolytic current of 160mA.

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectra of the CNPs (a) and the CNSs (b). (B) The fluorescence spectra of the CNPs (a) and the CNSs (b). The CNPs were concentrated for 10 times and the CNSs were diluted for 10 times. The insets were the digital photos for the products illuminated with white light (A) and UV lamp (B).
The XPS spectra in C 1s region of the CNPs (A) and CNSs (B).

The stability of the CNPs and CNSs. (A) the fluorescence spectra of the CNPs without (a) and with (b) the centrifugation for 60min, (b) the UV–vis absorption spectra of the CNSs without (a) and with (b) the centrifugation for 60min, The centrifugation speed was 10,000rpm. The excitation wavelength was 330nm. The absorbance was measured at 230nm.

Effect of the electrolytic current. (A) Absorbance of the flowing electrolytes and purge fluids which obtained in different electrolytic current. (B) Fluorescence intensity of the flowing electrolytes and purge fluids which obtained in different electrolytic current. The purge fluid was diluted for 10 times.

The fluorescence spectra of the electrolytes which were obtained with the normal electrolysis and flowing electrolysis. The electrolytic currents were 10mA (A) and 160mA (B). All the electrolytes were diluted with pure water to 300mL.

The potential application of the CNPs and CNSs. (A) The fluorescence spectra of the CNPs with different concentration of Fe3+. The concentration of Fe3+ was 0 (a), 1 (b), 2 (c), 5 (d), 10 (e) and 20 (f)×10−6 molL−1. (B) DPVs of 4×10−6 molL−1 acetaminophen on bare GCE (a) and CNSs modified electrode (b).

head.ce:title: Flowing electrolytic synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and carbon nanosheets

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A flowing electrolytic device equipped with large electrode area and high electric field intensity was designed for the preparation of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) and carbon nanosheets (CNSs). Comparing with normal electrolysis, the flowing electrolysis showed higher efficiency for the preparation and separation of the products. In the high intensity electric field, the CNPs and CNSs were enriched to the flowing electrolyte and the anode surface respectively. The obtained CNPs were regarded as a fluorescent probe of Fe3+, and the CNSs modified electrode was fabricated to be an electrochemical sensor of acetaminophen for its electrocatalytic properties. So the CNPs and CNSs were expected for the application of fluorescent and electrochemical detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical method is a traditional route to produce carbon-based nanomaterials [1–5]. In the most cases, carbon-based materials such as graphite anode were electrochemically oxidized in the electrolyte. According to the literature [6,7], abundant carboxyl groups were generated in the anode surface. The carboxyl groups reduced the interaction of the graphite layers, and increased the hydrophilicity of the graphite layers. So, the oxidized graphite layers easily exfoliated from the graphite surface. The exfoliated graphite layers have hydrophilic carboxyl groups and hydrophobe graphite surface. For the π–π interaction and hydrophilic interaction, the small exfoliated graphite layers tended to wrinkle in the form of CNPs [8], the large exfoliated graphite layers tended to be the other products such as carbon nanoribbons, carbon nanosheets and graphene sheets. However, the carbon-based anode was not necessary. The fluorescent CNPs also could be prepared by the electrooxidation of ethanol with the inert electrode [9]. Compare with the other preparation methods such as carbonization synthesis [10–13], hydrothermal synthesis [14–17], microwave synthesis [18–20], ultrasonic synthesis [21–23], and chemical methods [24–26], electrochemical method shows some unique advantages such as simple operation and low cost.

However, the electrochemical method has also met some defects such as by-product and residual salt. In order to get enough products, the electrolysis were always sustained for several hours unless the color of the electrolyte turned to brown or black [27,28], and the product was the mixture of different carbon nanomaterials [29,30]. In addition, the salts in electrolyte were also kept in the product. So, it is necessary for the separation of the nanoscale products and the removal of the salts in the electrolyte by long time dialysis [6,7]. In our previous work [31], the sonoelectrochemical method was proposed for the simple preparation of pure blue fluorescent CNPs by combining the high intensity electric field and ultrasonic field. The complex purification was avoided in this method because the deionized water was used as electrolyte. But it was hard to enlarge the scale of the sonoelectrochemical preparation for the limited electrode area.

Flowing electrolysis is an efficient electrochemical method. In this method, the electrolyte flow through the electrode surface. According to its high mass transfer rate and refresh electrode surface, the flowing electrolysis has been used for waste water treatment [32,33], electrochemical detector [34,35] and reaction kinetics study [36–38]. Herein, a novel flowing electrolysis method was developed for the preparation of carbon nanomaterials. Compare to our previous work, the high intensity electric field was retained because it was necessary for the electrolysis in deionized water. But the ultrasonic electrolysis was replaced with flowing electrolysis. The special designed flowing electrolytic device has a large electrode area, and the flowing deionized water which was used as electrolyte kept washing the electrode surface. Two kinds of carbon nanomaterials with different morphology such as CNPs and carbon nanosheet(CNSs) were generated and separated in the flowing electrolytic device, and their properties and potential applications were also discussed.

The electrolysis was occurred in a homemade flowing electrolytic device as shown in Fig. 1 . The anode of the flowing electrolytic device was a graphite cylinder. Its diameter and height were 18mm and 100mm, and its working area was 5600mm2. The large anode area was beneficial to generate more products and convenient for further enlargement. The graphite anode was placed in the center of a titanium tube cathode. The inner diameter and height were 20mm and 102mm. Hence, the gap between the anode and the cathode was only 1mm. The narrow gap leads to a high intensity electric field. The high electric intensity enabled that the electrolysis occurred in deionized water. The deionized water which flowed through the narrow gap was used as electrolyte. Its flow rate was 1mLmin−1.

Constant current method was applied in the flowing electrolysis. After the flowing electrolysis for 5h, the flowing electrolyte turned into a light yellow solution. The light yellow solution was collect as one product. Then the electrolytic device was purged with 12mL deionized water, the dark brown purge fluid was collected and centrifuged for 10min (10,000rpm) to remove the black precipitate. The supernatant fluid of the purge fluid was also collected as another product.

The graphite anode was spectrum pure. All other chemicals were of analytical grade. The electrolytic voltage was supplied from a BG-Power 600i DC power source. The flowing electrolyte was pushed with a SHIMADZU LC-20A high performance liquid chromatography pump. A TECNAI G2 transmission electron microscope (TEM) was used to investigate the surface morphology of the electrolytic product. A NICOLET AVATAR 330 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer and an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) were used to investigate the surface groups of the electrolytic products. The X-ray photoelectron spectrometer was equipped with the Mono AlKα X-ray radiation as the source for excitation at a pressure of less than 2×10−9 mbar in the chamber. The Raman spectra were measured by a DeltaNu Raman spectrometer (model Inspector Raman, its diode laser excitation wavelength is 785nm). The fluorescence spectra were measured by a SHIMADZU RF-5301 spectrofluorometer. The UV–vis spectra were measured by a PGENERAL T-6 spectrophotometer. The optical images were shot by a PANASONIC TZ-3 digital camera. The zeta potentials were measured by BROOKHAVEN 90Plus/BI-MAS Zeta potentiometer.

The electrochemical experiments were performed with a CHI-832 electrochemical analyzer with a three electrode system. A glassy carbon electrode (GCE, Ф =3mm) was used as working electrode, a platinum foil was used as counter electrode and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was used as the reference. The electrolyte solution was 0.2molL−1 PBS (pH=7.4) unless otherwise noted. The bare GCE was polished with 0.05μm gamma alumina and rinsed ultrasonically with redistilled water. The modified electrode was prepared by casting 5μL CNSs dispersion on the GCE surface and dried under an infrared lamp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The design of the flowing electrolytic device
ce:section.ce:para: The main characteristic of the flowing electrolytic device were the high electric field intensity and the large electrode area. The narrow gap between the anode and the cathode was designed for the high intensity electric field. The high electric field intensity could overcome the huge resistance of the deionized water and the thick electric double layer on the electrode surface. So the electrolysis could occur in the deionized water. In the high intensity electric field, the graphite anode was oxidized in this flowing electrolytic method. The oxidized graphite fragments were exfoliated from the graphite surface as the products. So, a large electrode area was contributed to generate more electrolytic products. Our design of the electrolytic device had a large anode, and it was convenient for further enlargement.

The flowing electrolyte and the purge fluid were collected as the electrolytic products. Their morphology was investigated with TEM. In the TEM images, the main products were CNPs and CNSs. The product obtained in the low electrolytic current contained more CNPs; and the product obtained in the high electrolytic current contained more CNSs. In addition, the CNPs and CNSs were not equally distributed in the flowing electrolyte and the purge fluid. The CNPs were enriched in the flowing electrolyte, and the CNSs were enriched in the purge fluid. As shown in Fig. 2 , when the electrolytic current was 10mA, the TEM image demonstrated that the flowing electrolyte was mainly in the form of CNPs, the scale of the CNPs was about 10–20nm. The purge fluid was the mixture of CNSs and CNPs, the scale of the CNSs was about 200–300nm. When the electrolytic current was 160mA, the flowing electrolyte was a mixture of CNPs and CNSs, and the purge fluid was mainly in the form of CNSs with a scale of 300–500nm. Hence, the pure CNPs and CNSs could be prepared in the different electrolytic conditions. When the expected product was the CNPs in the flowing electrolyte, the 10mA current should be chosen; when the expected product was the CNSs in the purge fluid, the 160mA electrolytic current should be chosen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of the CNPs and CNSs
ce:section.ce:para: The different UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescent properties of the CNPs and CNSs were also been investigated. In Fig. 3 A, the CNPs were almost colorless and the CNSs were dark yellow. Even the major peaks appeared at 230nm (K absorption band), their UV–vis spectra exhibited obvious difference in visible region. While the CNPs hardly showed any absorption, the CNSs showed obvious absorption in the visible region. The fluorescence spectra of the CNPs and CNSs were shown in Fig. 3B. The maximum of their fluorescence emission spectra were at 450nm, while the excitation wavelength was fixed at 330nm. The fluorescence intensity of CNPs was much higher than CNSs. The CNPs showed weak absorption and strong fluorescence emission, and the CNSs showed strong absorption and very weak fluorescence emission.

The surface groups of the CNPs and CNSs also showed obvious difference. In Fig. 4 A , the C1s region of the CNPs’ XPS consisted of two peaks located at 284.8 and 288.6eV, corresponding to the CC and COOH bond. In Fig. 4B, the C1s region of the CNSs' XPS consisted of four peaks located at 284.8, 286.1, 287.3 and 289.0eV, corresponding to the CC, CO, CO and COOH bond. The CNPs had more carboxyl groups than the CNSs, and the CNSs have more kinds of oxygen-containing groups than CNPs. The FTIR data (Fig. S1) also support this result. The most characteristic features of the CNPs and CNSs’ FTIR were the absorption bands corresponding to the CO of carboxyl stretching at about 1625cm−1, the O-H deformation vibration at about 1420cm−1, the CO stretching of epoxide group at about 1080cm−1. The OH stretching vibrations of the OH group of CNPs and water appeared at 3432cm−1 as broad adsorption bands. However, the carboxyl's stretching peak of CNPs is higher than that of CNSs, it also indicated that the CNPs had more carboxyl groups than the CNSs.

The Raman spectra of CNPs and CNSs were also investigated. As shown in Fig. S2, the CNPs’ Raman spectrum was totally covered by the fluorescence. However, the CNPs’ Raman spectrum exhibit two strong peaks at 1330cm−1 (D band) and 1625cm−1 (G band). The D band was much higher than G band, it indicated that the CNSs has large amount of defects. Obviously, the defects were related to the abundant oxygen-containing groups on CNS's surface.

The CNPs and the CNSs were very stable in the water dispersion. As shown in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence intensity of CNPs and the absorbance of CNSs would not reduce after 60min of continuous centrifugation at 10,000rpm. The emission peak of CNPs and CNSs would not shift when excitation wavelength was varied (as shown in Fig. S3). The quantum yield of the CNPs was observed to be 2.0% (as shown in Fig. S4).

The flowing electrolytes and purge fluids' absorbance and fluorescence intensity were also changed with the electrolytic current. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the absorbance of flowing electrolyte and purge fluid increased with the electrolytic current. It meant that more products were generated in the high electrolytic current. As shown in Fig. 6B, the fluorescence intensity of the flowing electrolyte also increased with the electrolytic current, but the fluorescence intensity of the purge fluid obviously decreased in the high electrolytic current. It supported that the high electrolytic current contribute to the generation of more CNSs than CNPs.

Comparing to the normal electrolysis in the resting electrolyte, the flowing electrolysis showed higher efficiency. As shown in Fig. 7 , the fluorescence intensity of the flowing electrolyte was higher than that of the resting electrolyte. It indicated that more products were generated in the flowing electrolysis.

Besides the higher efficiency, the flowing electrolysis method exhibited more ability for the separation of the products. In the normal electrolysis, the product was a brown or black mixture of various scales of carbon fragments. After the complex purification such as dialysis and ultrafiltration, the CNPs were collected as the product and the CNSs were discarded as a waste. In the flowing electrolysis, the carbon fragments with different scales could be separated. The CNPs were enriched in the flowing electrolyte, and the CNSs were enriched in the purge fluid.

The separation of the CNPs and CNSs could be attributed to the electrophoresis effect. Both of the CNPs and CNSs were negative charged because of their abundant oxygen-containing groups. As mentioned above, there was a high intensity electric field in our flowing electrolytic device. When the CNPs and CNSs were generated in the surface of graphite anode, they were separated based on the electrophoresis effect. Their zeta potentials (ζ) had been measured. The CNPs’ zeta potential was about −22mV, and the CNSs' zeta potential was about −31mV. So, the CNPs with lower absolute value of zeta potential could easily escape to the flowing electrolyte. However, the CNSs were continually bound on the anode surface until it was purged in absence of the high intensity electric field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential application of the CNPs and CNSs
ce:section.ce:para: The CNPs and the CNSs showed excellent fluorescent and electrocatalysis properties. The CNPs could be used as a fluorescent probe. As shown in Fig. 8 A, in present of trace amount of Fe3+, the CNPs' fluorescence intensity decreased observably. The decrease of fluorescence intensity followed the Stern–Volmer formula. The linear range varied from 1×10−6 molL−1 to 2×10−5 molL−1 (inset of Fig. 8A), and its limit of detection was 1×10−7 molL−1 (S/N=3). So the CNPs could be used for the detection of Fe3+.

The CNSs were used to modify the GCE, and the modified electrode showed excellent electrocatalytic property. As shown in Fig. 8B, the oxidative peak of acetaminophen exhibited a 12-fold enhancement on the CNSs modified electrode comparing to bare GCE. The anodic peak current was proportional to the concentration of acetaminophen. The linear range was from 2×10−6 to 2×10−5 molL−1 (inset of Fig. 8B), and its limit of detection was 5×10−7 molL−1 (S/N=3). Thus, the CNSs were suitable for the modification of carbon-based electrode. In addition, the property of CNSs was similar to the carboxylic carbon nanotube, but the preparation of CNSs was much easier than the carboxylic carbon nanotube.

The CNPs and CNSs were generated by the flowing electrolytic method. Comparing to normal electrolysis, the flowing electrolysis showed higher efficiency and for the preparation and separation of the products. In the high intensity electric field, the CNPs and CNSs were separated based on their different zeta potential. The obtained CNPs were regarded as a fluorescent probe of Fe3+. The obtained CNSs showed excellent electrocatalysis property. So the products were expected for the application of fluorescent and electrochemical detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China for financially supporting this research under grant numbers 21127008 and 21105133, and Major National Scientific Instrument and Equipment Development Project (2011YQ03012409), respectively.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2014.12.151.
